TextENGNew: She had finished her last task of the day and was heading down the hallway when the sharp, confident click of footsteps drifted around a nearby corner.
(... Could that be Sis?)
It was only a hunch, but she stopped and held her breath to listen.
Then came a small, clear voice returning a greeting — "...Good work today."
It was exactly who she had expected. Madoka snapped her fingers in satisfaction.
(Since I'm here, might as well say "good work" myself.)
Before long, the presence of that person drew close to the corner.
A mischievous impulse rose in Madoka — she'd give her a little scare when they rounded the corner. She cleared her throat and drew a breath.
"Good woork for the day, Assistant Director Minai."
"...Wh-whaaaaaa?!"
Madoka had finally gotten used to using that title, but the moment she laid eyes on the person, she let out a shriek instead.
And the few officers walking and chatting nearby all likewise froze, their conversation dying mid-step.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "W-what happened to you, Sis? Why do you look like this?!"
"Come on, Mado — I mean, Minai-san. I've told you so many times, you have to keep your private and professional life separate at work—"
"Now is not the time for that!! What on earth happened to your clothes?! And how are you so injured?!"
The anger in Madoka's voice was entirely understandable.
The person standing before her — her sister Tomoe — looked absolutely *terrible*.
To put it plainly... it was awful. Atrocious. One sleeve of her jacket was torn clean off, and the blouse beneath had been ripped open.
The bare skin exposed beneath was streaked with red cuts and angry welts.
Her hair was in disarray, a red stain that was clearly blood had spread across her collar, and a bandage was pressed to the corner of her mouth.
On top of everything, a small wad of tissue was stuffed into one of her nostrils.
"...Oh, a little incident. I was tailing that key witness, and somehow it turned into a fight... and this happened. It looks worse than it is. I'm fine."
"It is a big deal! Look at your jacket, it's completely in tatters! And with your arms and legs all cut up like that — what if you scar?!"
"Hmm... I wonder if workers' compensation will cover it?"
"Forget workers' comp, worry about life insurance! Didn't you tell me last night today's stakeout was boring and perfectly safe?! So what is all this?!"
"It was safe... up until a certain point. Something unexpected happened and I had no choice but to press forward — you understand. ...Oh, it came out."
As she spoke, the wad of tissue slipped loose from her nostril and tumbled neatly into her upturned palm.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: ...The bloodstain below her nose looked both pitiable and absurd.
(What a sight she is...)
She almost buried her face in her hands — one woman desparing at another.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: ...It might be sisterly bias, but Madoka had always thought Tomoe fell unmistakably into the category of "beautiful women."

Her close-cropped hair gave her an androgynous air, but her refined, intelligent features — the kind you might see on a news anchor or corporate secretary — drew the eye regardless of whether the admirer was a man or a woman.
changeReason: Changed 'female analysts' to 'news anchor'; JPN 女子アナ means 'female announcer/anchor' - new translation is more accurate

TextENGNew: Since her arrival at this station, the number of bachelors — superiors, subordinates, and colleagues alike — who secretly harbored ambitions toward her was far beyond what two hands could count.
...And yet here she was, looking so thoroughly disreputable that she could pass for a third-rate stage comedian.
If any of those admiring men got a look at her now, a few might end up not merely disillusioned, but losing their faith in women altogether.
"I... I see nothing...! Nothing at all...!!"
"Th-this is a dream... Everything I see right now is a dream...!"
Indeed, some of the men were already shuffling away with their eyes averted, muttering something under their breath like a prayer.
Let us hope this strange trauma does not permanently warp their future perceptions of women. ...Amen.
"Honestly...! Even if you are a chief inspector, fighting a criminal hand-to-hand is reckless for a woman. It's practically suicidal."
``You're not Lord Mito. A suspect isn't going to throw himself on the ground and stop resisting just because you flash a police badge! Do you understand?!``
changeReason: Cultural reference changed: 'elders in Mito' to 'Lord Mito' (Mito Komon); 'opponent' changed to 'suspect'

TextENGNew: "I understand, I understand... and stop going on about Mito. I told you, this time it was a fluke."
"I can't take it if this keeps happening! What is this, a UN-designated conflict zone?!"
"I've never even been abroad..."
"It's a figure of speech!"
Then Madoka noticed Tomoe's detective partner standing behind her, and turned her anger in his direction.
"...Speaking of which, Fujita-san? Aren't you supposed to be here to restrain her and keep her safe? So why can't you do your job prop—... erly?"
...But after she'd started yelling at him, the strange look on his face made her cock her head in puzzlement.
The detective named Fujita... had drooped his shoulders in complete collapse, his face ash-white and drained, as though he had just returned from a tour of hell.
Or rather... it seemed like his very presence had grown so faint he might vanish at any moment.
"Umm... Fujita-san? ...Hello?"
"...It's useless. Right now, Fujita-kun has been hit with a torrent of words more terrifying than a death sentence and is nothing but a breathing corpse that won't respond."
Less and less of this made any sense.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "Well, it doesn't matter. I can't exactly go see the section chief looking like this, so I'm going to change. Sorry, Madoka, but could you please escort our zombie here back to the First Division office? ...Thanks."
``Ah, hold on — ...She's gone. And who can't separate work and personal life now~!``
changeReason: Narrative perspective changed from first person to third person

TextENGNew: Grumbling under her breath, Madoka pulled out her police notebook and jotted down today's date, the time, the location, and the slip Tomoe had just made.
On those pages, ever since Tomoe's assignment to Kakiuchi Station, was recorded every circumstance in which Tomoe had called her "Madoka" while on official duty.
No doubt Tomoe intended to use it as ammunition whenever they argued. ...What a waste of energy.
"Even so..."
"Uu, uuugh, Yukooo... I'm sorry, I was wrong, please forgive meee..."
"...Seriously, what happened here?"
With no sense of the full picture, Madoka let out a weary sigh.
October, 1982.
Nearly two months had passed since Tomoe transferred to Kakiuchi Station on a training secondment from the National Police Agency.
Tomoe's official title was Third Wide-Area Section Chief, First Division, Wide-Area Coordination Department, Chubu Regional Police Bureau, National Police Agency.

Impressively long. But in plain terms, she was a wide-area investigator responsible for coordinating and directing the prefectural police and local stations when a case crossed multiple prefectures and areas in the Chubu region.
And within Kakiuchi Station, she had been appointed to the title of "Assistant Director."
That ranked above section chief at a mid-sized station — making her number three in the hierarchy, just behind the chief and deputy chief. ...Allegedly.
However, the near-unanimous reaction of station staff upon seeing that official order was: "Of all things, how unfortunate..."
"...Isn't that taking things a bit too far? Making an elite quasi-career officer the 'Assistant Director' on her very first assignment here..."
"You're right. It's like finding out the destination of our 'field trip' is the mountain where they abandon old people..."
No surprise there — the title of "Assistant Director" appeared nowhere in the official police regulations, and the post carried a distinctly dubious reputation.
...It wasn't unusual for the National Police Agency to dispatch executive-track candidates to small and mid-sized stations for training.
The purpose was to let them advance their careers by experiencing real field investigations firsthand.

However, candidates of that type tended to be reasonably knowledgeable but overburdened by an elite sense of entitlement, and they had a way of meddling and preaching about rules and logic at every turn.
To make matters worse, they were prone to antagonistic, score-seeking behavior while simultaneously trying to promote themselves out of ambition — making them a nuisance of the highest order.
It could hardly be pleasant for battle-hardened detectives with real field experience to be lectured and bossed around by some puffed-up young know-it-all.
So behind their backs, they called these candidates "field-trip students" and privately mocked them while maintaining a surface veneer of compliance.
Naturally, the feelings of the field detectives when Tomoe first arrived were not entirely favorable toward her either... though the fact that she was young and beautiful did soften things somewhat.
...Yet even they, when they heard about how Tomoe had been assigned to this Assistant Director post, couldn't help but feel a twinge of sympathy. "Isn't that pushing it a bit far?"
"...I see the 'Assistant Director' title is still being used. I thought they'd abolished it since it sat vacant for the last five years."
"Six years, wasn't it? The last person to hold it was old man Horiguchi, if I remember right."

"That's right, it's coming back to me. He happened to scrape through his promotion exam and make chief inspector, but he never produced results, so he stalled at assistant section chief. ...They gave him that title for the last year before he retired, just to let him retire with a bit of dignity."
"Ahh... I did hear something like that. The truth is, after old Horiguchi became Assistant Director, he used to sit at his desk every day with nothing to do but read the paper."
"No matter how useless the man was, it was just sad to watch him sit there with nothing to do all day."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Because it wasn't an official position, the holder had no authority to give orders to detectives within the station. And more to the point, it carried no actual duties.
Furthermore, while the position was nominally within the detective track, it commanded no subordinates. ...It was an honorary post in the truest sense — suspended in mid-air.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "No matter how good the pay, I could never take that Assistant Director role myself. But anyway, how did they end up making this kind of personnel decision?"
"It was Deputy Chief Hata's doing. Apparently his reasoning was that a position like assistant section chief would be too low for an elite candidate from the center."
"Too low or not, does that justify sticking her at the 'window seat'?"
In the normal course of things, a quasi-career detective at the chief inspector rank would be given a post on par with section chief within any criminal investigation division.
Indeed, when a large-scale investigation arose requiring a wide-area investigator, that person would be empowered to issue binding directives even to the station chief.
That was, of course... only when "that kind of case" actually occurred.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Regardless, placing someone in a role outside the regular chain of command was an extraordinary exception — one that could only be attributed to deliberate malice.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "Nothing to be done about it. She was personally recruited by Chief Yamaoki, wasn't she? ...Which means the deputy chief jumped on her immediately and had her 'sent away.'"
"Not this again... honestly. How does a station this small manage to have factions tearing each other apart? Isn't it just ridiculous?"
"Absolutely."
...Even so, no matter what they privately thought, no one dared voice an objection openly.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: It was ultimately someone else's affair, and sticking their necks out to defend her would only invite the displeasure of those above them.
Besides, once Tomoe's training period ended, she would return to the National Police Agency. Any stand on principle would count for nothing...
So they watched the whole situation from a distance without getting involved, and speculated about how it would all unfold in the manner of people placing bets.
"...So what do you think this 'field-trip student' is going to do?"
"Well, the obvious route would be to go crying to the brass at the Chubu Regional Bureau. Then get reassigned to a different station — that's how it usually plays out."
"But that would mean the chief who went out of his way to bring her in for training loses face, wouldn't it?"
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "My bet is she'll ride out whatever achievements she can piggyback on, treating the whole thing as a nice long vacation. That's all anyone from above ever asks for — nobody wants real field work."

"I don't know. Career women can be aggressive about asserting themselves in male-dominated spaces, and self-consciousness tends to drive them. She might push hard in every direction and end up spinning her wheels magnificently."
"So... you'd step in to comfort her once her pride is in tatters and talk her into going out with you? ...You've been watching too many dramas."
"Oh, come on. She might be a bit intense, but she's at a pretty high level, wouldn't you say?"
"Well, that aside — there are already quite a few men I know of who have their eye on her. Who'll be the first to get shot down? That's something to look forward to."
"Ha ha ha ha..."
...However, as it turned out, the arbitrary predictions those detectives had made would be proven wrong in every particular.
Tomoe was not the pale-complexioned, inflated-head type so common among the elite.
Nor was she a hysteric or a coward... She was elite in the truest sense of the word.
"...That girl's done another outstanding job, has she. Infuriating, isn't it, Kojima-kun?"
"Indeed it is. This has exceeded our expectations somewhat."
"Hmm. 'Exceeded expectations' — I'd say I'm simply impressed by her run of bad luck."
Second Division Section Chief Okabe leaned back irritably in his armchair and tipped down the last of his lukewarm tea.
Standing at his side with an air of obsequious deference was his right-hand man — or rather, his flunky — Deputy Section Chief Kojima.
Kakiuchi City, its population swelling in recent years as a bedroom community, sits at the heart of the region thanks to the construction of Kakiuchi Airport, the largest in the Chubu area.
The Kakiuchi Station, responsible for the entire city's jurisdiction, handled a high volume of cases, and as a result the criminal investigation department had been split in two.
One was the "First Criminal Division," led by the craftsman-natured Section Chief Yamamura.
The other was the "Second Criminal Division," with Section Chief Okabe at its head.

For the time being, the former handled violent crimes and property offenses, while the latter dealt with intelligence cases and organized crime — though in a city of this scale, the boundary between them was difficult to draw clearly.
In practice, the two divisions competed over territory and credit, and the detectives on either side viewed those on the other with contempt. They were, as the saying goes, like cats and dogs.
Which meant that any time the First Division's reputation rose, Okabe, as head of the Second, could not help but find it deeply disagreeable.
"The man they caught today is a key figure in that smuggling ring, I hear. ...They'll be crowing about this just like last month. What exactly were the Second Division people doing?"

"Ah, yes. ...At present, the investigators of the Second Division have been conscripted to handle various administrative duties for the joint investigation headquarters that prefectural police headquarters set up the other day. The majority are fully occupied with that. As a result, progress on other investigations has fallen behind..."
"All right, I see. That's enough."
Okabe spat the words out bitterly.
...After all, it was he who had pushed aggressively to have the Second Division take on the prefectural headquarters' liaison work in the first place, hoping to earn himself some points.
So hearing that used as an excuse for the delays made it sound suspiciously as though the delays were his own fault, and that sat very poorly with him.
"...Just this month, the First Division has contributed to resolving three major unsolved cases that even prefectural headquarters couldn't crack."
"And there are at least four additional cases where the arrests were handed over to headquarters detectives, leaving the First Division's name off the record."
"Thanks to all this, Captain Hayami of the Mobile Investigation Team has apparently taken to calling her 'Princess' and is paying close attention to her work."
"'Princess,' is it. ...What an absurdly grandiose nickname."

"I believe it reflects a genuine acknowledgment of her ability. In fact, I hear that Section Chief Doujima from prefectural headquarters has unofficially approached Chief Yamaoki about formally bringing her into the First Investigation Division..."
"That's just polite table talk over drinks. A rumor-lover heard it and dressed it up in entertaining exaggerations. Prefectural headquarters is hardly short of talent."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: That said... setting aside her current performance, the likelihood of her being recruited to prefectural police headquarters after training was high. Even if it was only lip service, the fact that her name came up at all was proof she had attracted attention.
And even though Okabe had just used the word "luck" out of frustration, if skill were factored in as its own kind of luck, then she was unquestionably one of the most exceptional individuals here.
After all, her father was Detective Yuusuke Minai — a formidable name in the Metropolitan Police Agency's public security division, well regarded by superiors and subordinates alike.

He had lost his life in a fire accident eleven years ago, but his daughter Tomoe had taken up his legacy. Even as a rookie officer, she had drawn the attention of her father's former supervisors and colleagues, some of them quite powerful people.

Beyond that, she passed every promotion examination at the top of the list. Her language abilities were strong, and having majored in psychology at a national university, she was capable of explaining investigation policies in a clear, watertight, logically rigorous manner.

Furthermore, in the midst of her busy and demanding schedule, she had produced numerous papers on improvements to investigative methodology. The quality of those papers had led to her being recruited from prefectural police service into the National Police Agency itself. She was a woman of remarkable talent.

Indeed, her superiors' assessment was that she had the ability to pass even the Class I national examination. Had she taken that path, she would by now be serving not as a quasi-career officer but as a full career police bureaucrat.
changeReason: Narrative perspective changed from third person to first person

TextENGNew: In other words, the only bottleneck in Tomoe's career was the simple fact that she was a "woman." Strip that away, and there was nothing mysterious about her success.
(...So the 'recommended track' label was not just for show, then.)

The National Police Agency's "recommended track" — the so-called quasi-career path — was composed of specialists hand-picked from jurisdictional stations with the express purpose of cultivating expertise.
Having passed through that narrow gate, Tomoe was all but guaranteed an early promotion to superintendent or senior superintendent, barring some scandal.
Her situation was plainly different from Okabe's, who — try as he might — could only hope for a gracious promotion to superintendent in the final years before his retirement.
(In the not-too-distant future, will I find myself reporting to that girl...?)
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: The thought filled him with a bleak, somber gloom.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: The police force was a world where academic credentials and one's starting position mattered more than achievements and experience. And in matters of personnel, career advancement was often determined by factional affiliations and the balance of power.
In fact, the backing Tomoe received was said to include the endorsement of alumni from Kashima National University, her alma mater.
That being the case, Okabe could not free himself from jealousy and a sense of inferiority toward this blessed "girl." His irritation was deep and stubborn.

"'When the hen crows, the house shall fall' — it seems we have reached the end of days. The dignity of the police force has surely declined when a brazen young girl can brandish the sakura emblem of law enforcement."

"Be that as it may, her track record is undeniable. Even among our own Second Division staff, people are saying that Assistant Director Minai's insight and ability are on a different level from any 'field-trip student' they've seen before."
"...And is that your way of saying I lack an eye for talent?"
"Ah — no, no, nothing of the sort. I only meant that those were the words I overheard from people at the scene..."
Kojima fell into flustered backpedaling, and Okabe fixed him with a look of withering contempt.
...Though for someone as characteristically loyal as Kojima to say even that much meant the concern being raised was likely genuine.
In this station, Chief Yamaoki — transferred from the center a year ago — was clearly on poor terms with Deputy Chief Hata, a local man who had risen through the ranks on his own.
Rumor had it that Yamaoki's transfer had been forced through quite aggressively by central command, and that it had consequently eliminated Hata's internal appointment as the next chief.

In response to this, the station itself had split clearly into two factions: the detective divisions of Okabe and the Second Division aligned themselves with the "deputy chief's faction," while the general affairs and traffic divisions fell in with the "chief's faction." They clashed at every opportunity.
Meanwhile, the remaining First Criminal Division, under Section Chief Yamamura, had placed itself as a "neutral faction," maintaining a posture of disengagement from the factional struggle.
...Which was precisely why Hata feared, above all else, any development that might draw the First Division toward the chief's side.

Thus, the moment he learned that Tomoe — at the chief's own initiative — was being seconded to Kakiuchi Station and that a post assisting the First Division section chief had been prepared for her, he opposed it vigorously.

"...After all, however exceptional she may be, she is still young — and a woman. I think placing her in charge of specialists with strong individual characters and a craftsman's disposition is asking too much of her."
...And so on. He had no shortage of stated reasons.
However, Hata's true intention was to separate Tomoe — whom he regarded as belonging to the chief's faction — from the First Division, and thereby check Chief Yamaoki's expanding influence.
That said, the counterproposal of absorbing her into the deputy chief's faction by placing her in the Second Division was blocked by Okabe.
Her abilities were still an unknown quantity, and bringing aboard someone so inexperienced posed considerable risk; he was concerned that any trouble she caused might damage his own standing.
...Or more plainly: he had simply underestimated her.
As a result, at Hata's strong insistence, Tomoe was given the honorary "Assistant Director" post — a comfortable sinecure from which she could be kept safely out of the way...
(...Up to that point, everything had gone according to plan.)

However, the miscalculation of both Hata and Okabe was that Yamamura — whom they had taken for neutral — suddenly invited Tomoe to serve concurrently as the First Division's "Chief of Special Affairs," delegating investigative authority to her.

Incidentally, this title of "Chief of Special Affairs" was likewise a "specially appointed" position — one originally designed to be filled part-time by retired police officers acting as advisors to the section chief. ...Allegedly.
However, since it was intended for retirees rather than active-duty officers, there was very nearly no — in fact, absolutely no — precedent for an active officer being installed in the role...

"...I understand that Inspector Minai has considerable knowledge of Western-style investigative techniques. I thought this would be an excellent opportunity for our people in the field to see firsthand how effective such methods can be."
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'

TextENGNew: Whatever his reasoning, the outcome was that Yamamura withdrew entirely from investigative activities, effectively stepping back from the front lines — and the practical field leader of the First Division became Tomoe.
It was Yamamura's forceful rebuke of the overtly distorted personnel maneuvering by Hata and Okabe — and, it seemed, a bit of mischief on his part to see how this "field-trip student" would fare.

Yet against expectation, that decision proved well founded: the "behavioral analysis" methodology Tomoe introduced — identifying a suspect by analyzing patterns in their conduct — demonstrated its effectiveness within a short time.
Consequently, the First Division's clearance rate rose dramatically within a single month. Even Yamamura was reportedly stunned, his eyes wide at results that surpassed anything he had anticipated...
(All things considered, I should have taken her into the Second Division when I had the chance.)
changeReason: Narrative perspective changed from third person to first person

TextENGNew: He now bitterly lamented the misjudgment he had made at the time.

Had Tomoe been placed in the Second Division, the department would have had a capable assistant section chief rather than someone like Kojima, who was only capable of following orders — and the evaluations would have been reversed entirely.
Or even if things had played out differently, he would at least have been able to place limits on her freedom of action.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Not that this entire line of thinking wasn't profoundly backward-looking, as Okabe himself was aware...
"...In any case, Section Chief Okabe."
"What is it?"

"Well — it's something I've been puzzled about for quite some time. Why exactly do Assistant Director Minai and Chief Yamaoki seem to be so at odds with each other? Thinking about it logically, even as a member of the chief's faction, her behavior toward him is..."
"That's simply a performance for those around her. After all, that girl's younger sister—"
"Achoo!"

Having changed her clothes and started down the hallway, Tomoe pressed a hand to her nose and sneezed. In the same instant, a faint burning odor rose at the back of her throat — the remnant of her earlier nosebleed — and she couldn't help pulling a face.
(...Someone is talking about me?)
The thought rose unbidden, and Tomoe shook her head at her own foolishness. Giving credence to superstitions was contrary to her principles.
"Haah, I'm back."
"Oh, welcome baaack."
She pushed open the door to the break room, and Madoka rose from one of the empty seats and called out to her.
...Already off duty, apparently — she had changed into civilian clothes.
"Hey, Madoka. You know it's against the rules to use police facilities out of uniform."
"...Honestly, and that's the first thing you say when you walk in? After I put in unpaid overtime listening to Fujita-san complain for an hour."
Madoka shrugged with a thin smile. ...The fatigue on her face suggested she had been subjected to a considerable amount of it.
"...Come to think of it, where is Fujita-kun?"
"Oh, he headed home for now. He said he was going straight to his wife and kneeling on the floor to beg her forgiveness."
"Haah, honestly. ...At least say a word before you leave, would you?"

"He couldn't face you after dragging you into the mess, right? He kept repeating, 'please apologize to the assistant director for me.' ...I understand how he feels, but you can't keep tormenting the man, you know."
"I haven't tormented him. Not yet."
She had actually intended to say something mildly cutting... but Fujita's complete and utter dejection made it impossible to be the demon about it.
(Well, never mind. He probably had no room to spare.)
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Muttering this to herself, Tomoe bought a can of coffee from the vending machine.
...For a moment she nearly pressed the "hot" button by reflex, reminding herself with a start that it was already autumn.
"Want one too, Madoka?"
"Hmm, I'm heading out to eat after this, but... sure, why not. Oolong tea, cold."
"Sure thing."
Tomoe inserted a coin and pressed the button for oolong tea.
A dull thud, and the can rolled out at the bottom. She picked it up and tossed it over with a flick of her wrist.
"Got it. ...Oh, how much do I owe you?"
"Don't worry about it. ...Consider it overtime pay from me."
"Hmm~... being paid a can of tea for sitting through an endless litany of complaints doesn't quite feel like fair value to me~."
Even as she said it, Madoka pulled the tab and took one, two long gulps. The cold sensation sliding down her throat felt like a small comfort.
"Well anyway... you had bad luck today too, Sis. Imagine being mistaken for an affair while you were in the middle of a stakeout with Fujita-san. ...What on earth were you doing?"
"Nothing strange happened, I assure you. ...It was simply very noisy nearby, so I had to lean close to his ear to speak... In other words—"
"It looked like a kiss, and his wife saw it. What a classic setup..."
Even hearing it afresh, it was a spectacularly absurd story.

Lately, growing suspicious of her husband's habit of claiming overtime and coming home late, she had begun to tail him. ...Up to that point, it wasn't entirely unthinkable behavior for a jealous wife.

But bursting into their secret rendezvous — which was in fact a stakeout — and proceeding to strike the suspected mistress, Tomoe, with full force on the basis of a complete misunderstanding... naturally, Madoka was at a loss for words.
Surely she could have demanded an explanation, or at least confirmed the facts, before resorting to violence...
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "Fujita-san's wife sounds like more than just a jealous type... quite the force of nature. Why on earth did he want to marry someone that intense?"
"They've apparently been childhood friends since elementary school. ...And don't say things like that. You're not exactly in a position to judge others, are you?"
"Ah, right, my mistake."

TextENGNew: Realizing she'd poked a hornet's nest, Madoka promptly withdrew the remark.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: After all, in the eyes of the world, Madoka's own choice of "partner" was far more open to criticism.
"Then at the coffee shop there was a fierce argument — a real scene — and just at that moment, the key witness happened to come in."
"And then Fujita-san carelessly let the word 'stakeout' slip out, and the witness realized there were police there and bolted. That set off a chase."
"Obviously I couldn't just let him go at that point. If he'd gotten away, he would certainly have gone into hiding and destroyed the evidence. It was all or nothing."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "Don't just brush past that part..."
Madoka let her shoulders drop dejectedly. ...As always, she had no sense of humor. Not even a little.
Still... Tomoe had won that desperate gamble.

The suspect had pulled a military knife while struggling violently, and that violation of the Swords and Firearms Control Law gave them grounds to search his pockets. As luck would have it, they found a business notebook and physical evidence inside.
They later learned he had stopped at the coffee shop to make a call on his way to a business meeting.

By handing over the arrested suspect to investigators from prefectural headquarters who arrived shortly afterward — though the timing was somewhat accelerated — they had managed to catch the actual drug transaction in progress and apprehend a notorious figure.
changeReason: 'culprit' changed to 'suspect'

TextENGNew: "...But Fujita-san was going through absolute hell at that point, wasn't he? What was it like?"

"Hmm~, by the time I got back, his wife was already gone... and Fujita-kun was sitting on the floor hugging his knees. According to the manager, she had launched straight into the classic 'I'll kill you and then disappear'—"
"Well, wouldn't it be more like 'I'll die too'? Where exactly is she planning to disappear to?"
She slipped the quip in, then let out a long sigh.
Fujita was being Fujita, but Sis was being Sis too. It was all conjecture, but Madoka could imagine the psychological state of both parties.
Certainly, if you saw your husband in what appeared to be a warm, intimate moment with a woman more attractive than yourself, panic was an understandable response — a crush of defeat and inadequacy.
(Old-fashioned in her thinking, and yet utterly unaware of how beautiful she is.)
Her sister's one true flaw was having no idea whatsoever what level she was operating at, in terms of appearance.

TextENGNew: Which was precisely why it never occurred to Tomoe that she could be swept up in a romantic misunderstanding. If only she had a slightly stronger sense of herself as a woman...
"...Still, good work catching the man. The suspect was a former prefecture-level track-and-field athlete, wasn't he?"
changeReason: 'culprit' changed to 'suspect'

TextENGNew: "If you're counting track and field, I've done track and field too. And I have the advantage in youth and stamina."
"...High jump and stamina — are those actually related?"
"That was a joke. ...Just let it go."
"Your jokes are so hard to read, Sis..."
Even as she said it... Madoka thought to herself — too embarrassed to say it aloud — well, this is just who Sis is.

For an ordinary detective, tracking down and cornering a suspect with an intimate knowledge of the terrain — and one who had broken through a perimeter — over the course of several hours would have been impossible.
And when the cornered suspect drew a military knife, choosing to engage bare-handed without even a baton was plainly reckless. Yet she had actually subdued him. That really was remarkable.
changeReason: 'opponent' changed to 'suspect'

TextENGNew: (That doesn't mean I have any intention of following her example, though.)
Sis was Sis. And Madoka was herself.
Her sister seemed to feel the same way — she never imposed her values about police work on others. Madoka was grateful for that.
"...By the way."
"Hm?"
"You mentioned a little while ago you were going out to eat tonight. ...Who with?"
"Urk."
"There's no chance you're eating alone...@k@vS11/28/420100052. is there? Not you."
"Ah, ahahahaha..."
She managed an awkward laugh and tried to cover, silently berating herself.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: As expected of the keenest mind Kakiuchi Station had to offer — this "Princess" made a single careless word a matter of life and death...!
"Umm, that is... you know?@k@vS11/29/420400073. So..."
"Just so you know — if you lie to me, I will be upset."
"B-but what if I tell the truth...?"
"That depends on what the truth is. ...However, there are 'certain circumstances' that would make me upset regardless."
"Well, umm..."
Fairly certain that "certain circumstances" described her situation exactly, Madoka found herself darting looks in every direction, completely stuck — unable to say either the truth or a lie.
...Aah, if only they could at least agree on this one point!
(A plausible escape route, a plausible escape route... @k@vS11/29/420400077. There is none~!)
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: She briefly considered throwing out a colleague's name — anyone at all — but immediately abandoned the idea. She had tried that trick before, and the truth came out the very next day. ...The fallout had been brutal.
If anything like that happened again, Madoka knew she would regret not at least coordinating their stories beforehand...

"...S-Sis, you've got it wrong. He's really not a bad person, I promise — that behavior is just on the surface, deep down he's completely—. And the restaurant we're going to tonight is actually a very respectable place..."
...No use. Whatever she said, it wasn't going to work.
With that grim realization, Madoka had already started thinking of how she would apologize to the person waiting for her ahead — Yamaoki...
"...Well. That's fine."
...But, unexpectedly.
Tomoe asked nothing more, and let the matter go with surprising ease. The reaction left Madoka staring at her, blankly.
"Are you all right for time? If you'd like, I could drop you off somewhere nearby."
"N-no, that's... not necessary. But... is it really all right? For me to go?"
"What do you mean, all right? You've already made a reservation, haven't you. It would be wasteful to cancel."
"Huh? Oh... yes. Then I'll go... okay?"
She nodded, though the look on her face was not half-convinced but rather in full-blown suspicion.
Something was wrong. She was being unusually understanding. Far too unusually.
It was well known within the station that Tomoe was vehemently opposed to Madoka's relationship with Chief Yamaoki and his evident hope of marrying her.

Just recently, an argument with Madoka had culminated in Tomoe marching directly into the chief's office and causing a scene, unleashing a torrent of outbursts including "Think about your age, you senile old fool!"
(At any normal station, that would have earned a disciplinary reprimand...)
So there was a nagging anxiety — that doing exactly as Tomoe suggested might mean walking straight into a trap.
"Sis,@k@vS11/29/420400090. ...you know who I'm going with, right?@k@vS11/29/420400091. Tonight's dinner... is with Kaoru-san."
"So?"
"N-no... I mean, so, umm."
...What could she do? She had absolutely no idea whether this was all a trap.
Should she take it at face value, thank her stars, and go? Or...
"Why are you reading so much into it. ...I'm simply saying, 'Have a nice time.'"
Tomoe heaved a sigh and turned to face Madoka. She shrugged her shoulders and turned the empty coffee can over in her hands as she spoke.
"Well... even between sisters, it's unseemly to meddle in someone else's love life. I've said what I wanted to say. The rest is up to your own judgment."
"...R-really?"

"As for marriage, I have reservations. But as for your relationship itself — I won't say another word. I can't exactly cheer you on, but I won't stand in your way. ...In the end, this is your life, Madoka."
A broad smile broke across Madoka's face, and she struggled to hold back the sudden welling in her eyes.
To anyone else, perhaps it wouldn't have seemed like a great deal.
But still... this was the first time, in any shape or form, that Tomoe had shown even a trace of acceptance toward the relationship.
Of course, it was all so sudden — a part of her couldn't quite process it.
And she still didn't understand what had brought about this change in her sister.
But even so, just hearing those words was enough to be grateful for. And she was happy...
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "Then I'll be off. Thank you, Sis."
"...Oh, Madoka."
"What is it?"
"...... No, never mind.@k@vS11/28/420100072. Take care."
"Yep!"
And Madoka left the break room at nearly a skip, her steps light and quick.
"...I couldn't ask her. Again."
She sank deep into the cheap armchair, leaned back, and turned her gaze to the ceiling.
The fluorescent light blinked and stuttered, the shadows of small flying insects drifting near it, then away.
They should have been burning — yet their instinct to seek light would not let them go. Tomoe watched them with a quiet, sad smile, stretched broadly, and exhaled a long breath.
"'Not a bad person,' you said.@k@vS11/28/420100076. ...I know that too. I've known him longer than you."

As Madoka was well aware, it was Tomoe who had known Yamaoki longer. If Madoka's relationship with him spanned two or three years, Tomoe's went back more than twice that... They had been acquainted before she ever put on a uniform.
"That's exactly why... even so."
The age difference, she could accept. It gave her pause, in various ways, but she could be persuaded.
But... the "rumor" that circulated about Madoka and Chief Yamaoki — was it actually true? Or a baseless lie?
Even now, Tomoe couldn't determine the truth, and it continued to weigh on her.
``...Was she cultivating the chief's favor — to advance her sister's career?``
changeReason: Narrative perspective changed from first person to third person

TextENGNew: To dismiss it as sheer slander was something Tomoe couldn't do with confidence.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: In fact: Tomoe had been selected for the National Police Agency's recommended track from Ibaraki Prefecture Police the previous summer. And Yamaoki had been appointed chief of Kakiuchi Station — and Madoka had been inducted as a police officer — the previous spring.
...The timing was close enough that not questioning the sequence would have been strange. And the content of the rumor carried its own persuasive logic.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "But... what are the actual facts?"
Had Madoka genuinely worked her way into Yamaoki's favor for her sister's sake?
Had Yamaoki, in response, lobbied upper management to have her selected for the recommended track...?
The narrative had the ring of truth, but Tomoe had never heard it directly from either of them — and the speculation felt too base, too vile, to raise herself.
And so she too was being turned about by the same rumors.
(Why did Madoka become a police officer in the first place...?)
When Madoka first announced that she intended to become a police officer, Tomoe had genuinely rejoiced — grateful that her sister had finally decided to take her life seriously.
But... after becoming aware of the rumors, she had begun to see it in a very different light.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Madoka had faced prejudice from the start of her career as a police officer, given that she had previously worked at a cabaret club.
And when it became common knowledge that she was in a relationship with Chief Yamaoki, she was subjected to curious scrutiny all the more.
But thinking it through simply: if the only purpose was to win the chief's favor, there was no need to become a police officer. If anything, doing so only created obstacles.
Knowing she would invite criticism and censure, why had she become a police officer anyway...?
"...Well. Whatever the answer is."
Tomoe had always found Madoka and Yamaoki's relationship — with its uncertain motivations — deeply uncomfortable, and had consistently pushed back against it.
But recently, at long last, she had come to understand that whatever the circumstances, Madoka genuinely cared for Yamaoki.
In which case... she had already told Madoka everything she could say as a sister.
All that was left was to trust that Madoka's eye for the person she loved was sound — that the rumors weren't true — and to watch over things as they unfolded...
"...Her lover, huh."
With no one around, she let herself murmur it to the empty room.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Back when she was a student, and even after joining the police, Tomoe had been confessed to by various men and had been in relationships — yet somehow she had never let herself grow close to anyone in any real depth. Not to this day.
...It wasn't that she disliked men, or found them difficult. And she had, in her own way, felt something like longing — a certain admiration — for someone, at various times.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: But for Tomoe, there was something that mattered more than any of that.
A purpose so grave that it occupied her completely, to the point that she never noticed — or minded — if she seemed different from those around her...
(So I want her to be happy. @k@vS11/28/420100084. Happy enough for both of us...)
She was certain she could not move forward until she had settled the past.
But she wanted her sister to live with her eyes on the future.
It might be a selfish wish — but there was no obligation for sisters to choose the same path in life...
Tomoe turned her eyes from the ceiling and opened the desk drawer, pulling out a file from inside.
Within it, pages carefully detached one by one from a notebook lay enclosed in a polyethylene bag.
And this was the one and only clue left behind by Tomoe's father, Yuusuke Minai...
Eleven years ago. 1971.
Sunday. Three weeks had passed since the fire that had killed both her parents and left her younger sister with a wound that would take years to heal.

Discharged from hospital after recovering from her injuries, Tomoe visited the charred remains of their home... With the permission of the police, she passed through the gate and stepped into the blackened garden.
Desperately holding back the anguish that threatened to crush her chest, and the rage that had nowhere to go, Tomoe turned her gaze toward the ruins.
The room she and Madoka had shared on the second floor had already collapsed... nothing remained but wreckage.
On the scorched veranda where their mother used to sit in the sun, a single sandal — probably hers — remained.
The clothesline pole was bent from the heat, twisted into an odd shape, lying on the ground.
The small shed where her father had stored the tools for his one beloved pastime, weekend carpentry, was leaning at a broken angle.
The persimmon trees she had planted with her mother... had died before bearing fruit even once.
...The fire had been deliberately set.
Her father didn't smoke, and she was utterly certain her meticulous mother would never have accidentally started a fire. Which was why the impact of learning the truth had been beyond measure.
Her clenched fists trembled.
A burning, acrid smell welled up from the back of her throat.
Why.
Why did they have to go through this.
What reason — what reason in this world — demanded that their ordinary happiness, just a family living in peace, be reduced entirely to ash in someone's moment of venting...!!
The insides of her eyes burned.
A high-pitched ringing tore through her head from deep inside her ears, and her consciousness began to feel very far away.
Hatred.
Despair.
changeReason: Emotion changed: 'Regret' to 'Despair' - different emotional meaning

TextENGNew: She wanted to kill whoever had done this to them...!
"...Damn it, damn it all—...!!"
The moment she cried out, the burning, acrid air surged again from the back of her throat.
Her field of vision began to waver, to blur... and the sense of distance dissolved as her consciousness retreated further.
Even so.
Even so... Tomoe did not cry.
She would not let herself sink to the bottom of this broken grief...
Like hell she would cry.
Like hell she would yield.
I will not bow down until the person who took everything from me has answered for what they did — until proper punishment has been carried out!
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: For what they did to Mother and Father, I will — I will certainly—!!
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Having struck the shed on impulse, Tomoe noticed something odd about the small gap that had opened up and leaned in to look.
She forced the sliding door open with all her strength, and tucked behind the toolbox hidden in the back... she found a single notebook wrapped in oilpaper.
"Kakiuchi Airport — June, Wednesday — Go."
On the final page alone, those words were written in large, conspicuous characters, circled to stand apart.
"No matter how many times I read it... I still don't understand what it means."
She exhaled a long sigh.
Back when Tomoe and her family had lived in Tokyo — what connection had her father had with Kakiuchi Airport?

Ever since she became a police officer and gained the authority to act, she had seized every opportunity to examine his case records or have them examined on her behalf... yet to this day, the answer had remained out of reach.
She pried the window blinds apart with her fingers and called softly out into the empty air beyond.
Her father had indeed visited that place — unmistakably — just a few days before the fire.
But why — and what exactly he had been investigating there — she had no idea whatsoever.
...Eleven years, since that day.
Still without anything that could be called evidence, only time had passed — silently, and for nothing.
